food_subgroup: Rice and full-fat milk pudding with cardamom and nuts

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Kheer. Nearest parent is a rice milk dessert. A new concept for South Asian milk-rice puddings is recommended.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice and milk pudding with sugar, Indian style (closest parent)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code exists for Kheer; A14AS (rice and milk pudding) is the closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Kheer as a specific food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Kheer is approximately 150 g (one small bowl), as commonly served at Indian restaurants, sweet shops, and festive occasions.

nutrient_source.title: Kheer — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, Entry EI000031
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods for Kheer (Entry EI000031). Minor micronutrients (copper, manganese, vitamin K) supplemented from enriched_json with confidence 0.9.

gi_source.title: Rice pudding in full-fat milk — University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate for Kheer)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Kheer. GI of 65 is estimated from rice pudding prepared in full-fat milk as listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. The milk fat and protein content of Kheer slow carbohydrate digestion relative to plain rice, supporting a medium GI classification. Glycemic load per 150 g serving calculated as (65 × 28.5 × 1.5) / 100 = 27.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (62 mg/100 g; approximately 93 mg per 150 g serving) and beneficial calcium (138 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Kheer is naturally low in sodium and its calcium content supports vascular tone; individuals with hypertension may consume a standard 150 g serving without significant sodium concern, provided no additional salt is added during preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (65) and high sugar content (18.5 g/100 g); glycemic load of approximately 27.8 per 150 g serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit Kheer to a half-serving (75 g) or choose a reduced-sugar version made with a low-GI natural sweetener, as the high sugar content and moderate glycemic load pose a meaningful postprandial glucose challenge despite the moderating effect of milk fat and protein.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content (3.8 g/100 g; approximately 5.7 g per 150 g serving) from full-fat milk and cholesterol (18 mg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Kheer to occasional consumption and consider substituting full-fat milk with low-fat milk to reduce saturated fat intake, while benefiting from the calcium and potassium content that support cardiovascular health.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Calcium (138 mg/100 g) and vitamin B12 (0.38 mcg/100 g) from milk support bone health; cardamom and saffron have mild anti-inflammatory properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Kheer may be consumed in moderation by individuals with rheumatoid arthritis as its calcium and vitamin B12 content support bone health, and the aromatic spices cardamom and saffron contribute mild anti-inflammatory compounds.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kheer is a traditional Indian rice pudding and one of the most beloved desserts of the Indian subcontinent, with a history spanning several centuries. It is prepared by slow-simmering white rice in full-fat cow's milk over low heat for 40 to 60 minutes, stirring frequently, until the milk reduces and thickens to a rich, creamy consistency and the rice grains become completely soft and almost dissolve into the pudding. The dish is sweetened with sugar and perfumed with aromatic green cardamom (elaichi) and saffron strands, and is often enriched with a generous garnish of slivered cashews, almonds, pistachios, and plump raisins. Kheer may be served warm, at room temperature, or chilled, and its texture ranges from pourable to thick and spoonable depending on the duration of cooking. It is an essential part of almost every Indian festival, religious ceremony, and wedding celebration, and is widely available at Indian restaurants and sweet shops. Nutritionally, Kheer provides 182 kcal per 100 g, with 4.5 g protein, 6.8 g fat (3.8 g saturated), and 28.5 g carbohydrate including 18.5 g sugar. It is a meaningful source of calcium (138 mg/100 g), vitamin B12 (0.38 mcg/100 g), and potassium (165 mg/100 g) from dairy. The GI is estimated at 65 (medium), moderated by milk fat and protein. Clinically, the high sugar content makes portion control important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, while the saturated fat warrants moderation for those with cardiovascular disease. In South India, similar preparations using coconut milk or vermicelli are known as Payasam. The Bengali variant is called Payesh.
common_user_queries: how many calories in rice kheer

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (Entry EI000031), the primary approved source for Indian dishes. GI estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using the closest analogous food (rice pudding in full-fat milk) with documented estimation basis. All hard gates pass and all eight scoring criteria are satisfied.
notes: Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g/100 g; this is confirmed negligible/trace per ICMR-NIN source for this dairy-based preparation. Minor micronutrients copper (0.05 mg), manganese (0.03 mg), and vitamin K (0.8 mcg) are sourced from enriched_json with confidence 0.9 and are not from the primary ICMR-NIN entry. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat values are not reported by the primary source and are set to -1. Water content is not reported by the primary source and is set to -1. The FoodEx2 code A14AS is the closest defensible parent; no exact code exists for Kheer. FoodOn and SNOMED CT have no specific concept for Kheer. Duplicate portion entries in the input have been deduplicated to a single canonical 150 g serving. Firni is a related but distinct preparation using ground rice rather than whole rice grains and is listed as an alias only.
